text: Both eight and 12 millimeter ports are utilized, and there is also an assistant port near the diaphragm.
You can see the port placement here on the da Vinci XI system.
We begin these operations by dividing the inferior pulmonary ligament up to the inferior pulmonary vein.
Level 9 and 8 lymph nodes are removed and sent to pathology to make sure they are not positive.
The lung is then retracted anteriorly and the parenchyma is dissected off the airway posteriorly.
It is critically important to delineate the right upper lobe bronchus takeoff from the bronchus intermedius and to dissect out completely the 11R lymph node or sump node.
The subcarinal lymph node packet is then removed next, completely unblocked, as you can see here.
The wristed instrumentation and superior visualization of the robotic platform really allows this to be completed as a packet.
The lung is then retracted inferiorly and we perform our suprahilar dissection, removing the level 10R lymph node off of the underlying pulmonary artery or truncus arteriosus branch here.
This dissection really allows us to also complete dissection between the truncus arteriosus and bronchus, which later allows for easier division.
We then switch to a fissure view and divide the posterior fissure with serial firings of the stapler after creating a tunnel.
This is directed posteriorly and our prior dissection of the level 11 sump node allows for easier stapling.
The underlying posterior ascending artery is then visualized and circumferentially dissected and divided with the assistance of a vessel loop as seen here.
The lung is then retracted posteriorly and the anterior mediastinal pleura is dissected.
The right upper lobe vein is encircled making sure to preserve the middle lobe vein.

The right upper lobe vein is divided with a vascular load stapler and then the underlying truncus arteriosus branch can be easily encircled thanks to our prior dissection and it is subsequently divided with the assistance of a vessel loop providing counter traction
and divided by a vascular load stapler.
The minor fissure is then completed.

We then proceed to perform the bronchial transection and switch to infrared or firefly imaging so that we can see the bronchoscope more easily and determine the proximal and distal extent of resection.
We then divide our distal bronchial margin, which here is the bronchus intermedius,
As you can see, this is done at a right angle to the longitudinal axis of the bronchus.
We do this with cold scissors and subsequently divide the prairie bronchial tissue.
Care is taken to not denude the blood supply on the lateral aspect of the airway.
We do the distal bronchial transection first because this allows our camera to be directed proximally to determine the area for proximal bronchial transection, which is done here.
And you can see the anterior aspect has already been cut and now we are dividing the posterior membranous airway.
Again, making sure to do this at a right angle to the longitudinal axis of the airway.

Once the distal and proximal bronchial margin is transected, the specimen is sent to pathology for analysis and if there is adequate margin between the tumor and bronchial margin, then the reconstruction commences.
The anastomosis is done with a V-lock suture, which is a unidirectional braided suture that is excellent for suturing tissues on tension.
It works really well for bronchoplastic and sleeve resections robotically.
Because it is unidirectional and barbed, it cannot be pulled backwards through the tissue and this works really well when bringing the two ends of the bronchus together.
The anastomosis is completed in fact with two V-lock sutures.

The first of this is placed at the cartilaginous membranous junction on the medial aspect of the bronchus and the anterior aspect or the cartilaginous aspect of the bronchus is completed in a running continuous fashion.
The anastomosis is also just done in a single layer as you can see here.
The barbed unidirectional nature of this suture allows for the bronchus to stay together after the suture is tightened.

We place a second B-lock suture also at the medial aspect of the membranous cartilaginous junction and this will be used later to complete the posterior aspect of the bronchus or the membranous portion of the anastomosis.
You can also see here that we utilized a Vicryl stay suture on the left lateral aspect of the screen to take off additional tension and to help bring the bronchus together.
The Vicral Stay Suture is also helpful to manipulate the airway back and forth and the robotic forearms are really useful in being able to do this easily by switching between the arms back and forth.
Here you can see the anterior cartilaginous anastomosis being done.

It's very important to follow the curvature of the needle and to bring together the edges of the bronchus after each throw so as to bring the bronchus together and relieve the tension in the anastomosis.
Here the second V-lock suture is being used to do the posterior aspect of the airway and to bring together the membranous portion of the two bronchial ends.

You can see here how the robotic platform really is unique in its wristed instrumentation and high-level optics, which really facilitates fine dissection and instrument handling in this area and allows for this anastomosis to be completed with relative ease.
You can see that the posterior bembranous airway is also completed in a running continuous single layer fashion.
As we approach the last few bites of the anastomosis, the suture is tightened further.
And the anastomosis is completed.
The robotic wristed instruments are also very useful in helping dynamically space out the bites so that dog ears are avoided at the end.
And here you can see the two V-lock sutures being tied together.
We also do tie together the Vicryl stay suture as well.
Once the anastomosis is complete, we perform an underwater leak test to identify any areas that require revision or additional suture placement.
In this case, the leak test was negative.
After this, we do also buttress the anastomosis with autologous tissue, which can be pericardium, pleura, or thymus.
Here is a bronchoscopic view of our anastomosis showing no areas for concern.
